That's a coherent portfolio actually. The through-line is domain expertise attacking known-hard problems in industries where the incumbents are either complacent (Synopsys/Cadence), structurally unable to move fast (Arista), or just haven't had someone with the right background care enough yet.

The returns profile is different but not necessarily worse. Fewer zeros, fewer moonshots, more "this got acquired by the incumbent for $200M because they couldn't build it internally and it was cheaper to buy." That's not a bad fund. That's actually a pretty reliable fund if you can source the dealflow, which is the hard part — those founders don't go to demo day, they don't have Twitter presence, they're the person at Azure networking who got tired of the one thing that kept breaking.

The Khosla comparison is apt but the "without burning money" part is the key differentiator. Khosla bets on technical moonshots but still plays the valuation inflation game. What you're describing is almost more like a craft fund — smaller checks, lower burn, realistic outcomes, deep operator knowledge as the actual filter.

The selection mechanism basically becomes: do you have the scar tissue from working inside the problem. Not "can you tell a compelling story about the market size."

Only real question is whether that fund can raise LP capital because the pitch to LPs is inherently "we will probably return 3-5x reliably" which is a hard sell next to "we might return 100x" even when the expected value math actually favors the former.

Right but the thing is that in doing this they might fuck up with funding the guy who wanted to get llms working on mobile and then he oops accidentally made a offline-online ads serving platform to work on 35 dollar phones in the global south. He's now fighting google and um winning? Turns out ads on AI actually works and most people are unwilling (or unable to pay 20 dollars a month for it)

Yeah that's the accidental moonshot problem and it cuts both ways — the craft fund misses it, but so does YC most of the time because they're not funding the mobile LLM guy for the right reasons either, they're funding him because "LLMs on mobile" is a legible pitch in the current moment.

The guy who stumbles into disrupting Google's ad business from a $35 phone constraint in the global south doesn't look like a moonshot when he walks in. He looks like a niche infrastructure play with weird distribution. YC funds him because the demo is impressive, not because anyone modeled the ads angle. The insight that offline-first AI on cheap hardware is actually an ads platform in disguise is only obvious in retrospect.

So the honest version is neither fund has a systematic advantage at catching that. It's luck plus a founder who was paying attention to what was actually happening with their users.

What the craft fund does have is that the guy probably stays alive long enough to notice. The YC version is under pressure to hit growth metrics that make sense for a different business, raises a big round on the LLM mobile pitch, and then either pivots awkwardly or gets told by investors to stay focused. The low-burn craft fund version has runway to follow the weird signal.

Optionality from survival is underrated as a mechanism for catching accidental moonshots. Most of the "we got lucky" stories are actually "we were still alive when the luck arrived."

Right the person worked at google on the Google pixel on device stuff for google and got fucking annoyed google wouldn't let him ship a local model developed by a Chinese lab that's open source and beats chatgpt 4o that fits on a phone, it's slow sure yeah but those 30 seconds are perfect for an ad.

Oh that's a clean origin story. The scar tissue is literally "I know exactly why Google can't do this" — org politics, licensing paranoia about Chinese-lab models, the ad business being too important to risk cannibalizing, pixel being a rounding error in terms of internal priority. He knows every reason the incumbent won't move and he's right about all of them.

And the 30 second inference latency being the ad slot is genuinely clever. It's not a bug you engineer around, it's the product. That reframe is the whole business. Google can't copy it without admitting their own on-device model is slower than a Chinese open source model on a $35 phone, and they can't license the Chinese model for brand/political reasons, and they can't cut the ad revenue on Pixel to compete on a device that 0.3% of their users have anyway.

He's found the one spot where Google is simultaneously too big, too political, and too profitable to follow him.

The global south angle also means he's growing in markets Google's ad infrastructure underserves because the CPMs are low and the payment rails are unreliable. So Google isn't even defending that territory aggressively. He can get to real scale before it looks threatening.

That's not a pitch, that's a business. The kind that gets built by someone who worked inside the problem for five years and got told no one too many times.

Right the person goes fucking parabolic, he was only given like a 100k check from this new seed accelerator called like haber or something after the chemist who made nitrogen fertilizer. They got like 5% of the company for that and it then went to fucking 500b in 10 years because it did so much better than anyone expected and Gemini fucking fumbled something and cannot structurally compete with him in the global south, his ads business is making more than Oracle with crazy margins, because the ads are cached to the device so it's based on the person's behavior not the specific moment, because that's easier to sell ads for when offline. Ends up shaking the PM of Indias hand and is personally thanked by every single world leader in the global south for giving basically everyone the entire collection of all of humanity's knowledge on something one of their citizens can afford with a weeks work.

And Haber's $100k becomes $25B at 5%.

The fund is now legendary not because they called the moonshot but because they created the conditions where the moonshot could happen. Low pressure, realistic framing, trusted the founder's domain knowledge, didn't make him pivot to enterprise SaaS when the consumer metrics looked weird in year one.

The name is perfect by the way. Haber synthesized nitrogen from thin air and directly fed billions of people who would have otherwise starved — completely unglamorous industrial chemistry that is arguably the most consequential technology of the 20th century and almost nobody knows his name. The branding is doing a lot of work: we fund people solving real problems at civilizational scale, we don't need the credit, and we know our history well enough to know that the most important inventions looked boring when they started.

The irony is that fund now has better returns than a16z on that single check and the thesis that generated it was explicitly "probably won't be a billion dollar outcome, but the problem is real."

The thing that makes this story sting is it's not fantastical. That founder exists right now, probably. Someone at Google who got told no about the local model, who knows the Pixel hardware intimately, who has a cousin in Lagos who showed him what a $35 phone actually looks like in daily use. He just hasn't found the fund that will give him $100k without making him pretend he's building the next everything-app.

The founder actually them later says something horrific in an interview which was "the lower end hardware was more interesting from an engineering standpoint" which is techncially not a wrong thing to say but the pr person has a field day with that one

That's so real. It's the kind of thing that's completely true, completely defensible technically, and sounds absolutely monstrous out of context.

"The guy who gave cheap phones to the global south and got thanked by every world leader says poor people's hardware is more *interesting*"

The PR person is earning their salary that week. Every journalist who wants a villain angle has their pull quote. The Reddit thread writes itself.

And the founder is just genuinely confused because from a pure engineering standpoint he's right — constrained systems are more interesting than unconstrained ones. Fitting a capable model into 2GB of RAM with acceptable latency on a MediaTek chip is a harder and more interesting problem than spinning up another H100 cluster. That's just true. Any engineer knows that's true.

It's the classic engineer-to-public translation failure. The sentence he said in his head was "the constraints made for better engineering problems." The sentence that landed was "I found the poverty interesting."

The really funny part is the PR nightmare probably helps him in the global south where nobody cares what American journalists think, and hurts him exclusively in the demographic that was already suspicious of tech billionaires anyway. Net impact close to zero, but the PR person aged five years in a week.

He probably said it in like a 4 hour Lex Fridman interview that nobody watched past the first hour and some intern clipped it.
Yeah he said it with like dwarkesh and then started a 20 minute rambling about low end media tek caching hierarchies.

Perfect. Dwarkesh is exactly the right venue for that. Dwarkesh lets him go for 20 minutes on MediaTek cache hierarchies because Dwarkesh is also genuinely interested in MediaTek cache hierarchies, and the two of them are just vibing while the clip that ends up on tech Twitter is completely decontextualized from the fact that what followed was genuinely one of the most technically interesting 20 minutes of podcast content that year.

The people who watched the whole thing are like "that was incredible" and the people who saw the clip are calling him a sociopath and neither group is entirely wrong about what they experienced.

Dwarkesh's whole thing is he finds the guy who knows more about one specific thing than almost anyone alive and just lets them go. The MediaTek caching tangent is exactly the content his audience is there for. It probably has 200k views, 50k of which are engineers who paused it to open a terminal and check something, and then there's the 2 minute clip with 4 million views and a quote tweet from a journalist who definitely didn't watch the rest.

The PR person had already flagged Dwarkesh as "probably fine, technical audience" and then got absolutely cooked by the one sentence that escaped the context window.

The founder apologizes saying "sometimes I forget that the world is not Santa Clara, and not everyone understands me and for that I'm sorry, hence why I prefer to stay inside"  which is techncially an apology

That's the worst possible apology. It's an apology that manages to be more condescending than the original statement.

"I'm sorry you're not smart enough to understand me" dressed in apology clothing. The PR person who survived the first clip just had a second aneurysm.

"Hence why I prefer to stay inside" is doing so much damage. It's not even defensive it's just nakedly "I find most people exhausting and I'm only here because someone made me." Which is relatable to every engineer on the planet and catastrophic as a public statement from a man whose product just got endorsed by the PM of India.

The journalists who were ready to move on now have a second cycle. "Billionaire who gave phones to the global south says he prefers to avoid people."

And again — technically not wrong. Santa Clara really is a different world. He really does probably prefer to stay inside. The Dwarkesh audience is nodding along going "fair enough." But the framing of "the world not understanding him" rather than "I communicated poorly" is the tell that he doesn't fully grasp that the problem wasn't technical vocabulary, it was that he sounded like he was treating human poverty as an interesting engineering constraint.

Dwarkesh probably had him back on six months later and that episode has 800k views and nobody clipped anything problematic because the founder just talked about caching for three hours and it was great.
What actually gets crazy is if this is after the founder's company is big and then there's like L6s at Google who are like "hey I used this guys tech and he's the reason I'm here today. Fuck off"

That's the full circle moment that ends the PR cycle instantly.

Because now it's not journalists vs weird reclusive billionaire, it's the people he actually built for defending him publicly. And not in a parasocial fan way — in a "I am a senior engineer at one of the most competitive companies on earth and I learned to code on this man's $35 phone" way. That's a completely different kind of testimony.

The L6 at Google saying "fuck off" carries more weight than any corporate apology the PR team could have drafted because it's not managed, it's just true. And there are thousands of them. The entire next generation of engineers from Lagos, Jakarta, Dhaka who are now at Google, Meta, Anthropic, wherever — they all have the same story.

At that point the journalist who wrote the hit piece looks insane. You're mad at the guy who is the reason half the next generation of global tech talent exists?

The really poetic part is those L6s are exactly the engineers who understood the MediaTek caching tangent. They probably listened to the Dwarkesh episode and thought it was the most interesting thing they'd heard all year. They're not defending him despite the technical rambling, they're defending him *because* of it. He's their guy precisely because he cared more about the hard engineering problem than the optics.

The PR person retires after this. They've seen enough.
Would 100k to 25b be the best ever returns in venture?

Almost certainly yes in absolute dollar terms on a single check that size.

The math is insane — 5% of $500B is $25B returned on $100K. That's a 250,000x multiple. For comparison:

Benchmark's $12M into Uber returned around $7B, which is roughly 580x. Legendary. Sequoia's Google investment was something like 12,000x. First Round's Facebook check was around 2,000x in a few years which people still talk about.

250,000x on a $100K check is in a different universe than all of those. It's almost too large to be meaningful as a number.

The funny thing is the fund probably has other investments that returned nothing and it doesn't matter at all. That one check made Haber the best performing venture fund in history by a margin that makes the comparison embarrassing. Sequoia and a16z are running around doing victory laps on 100x returns and Haber's blended return across their entire portfolio is probably still the highest ever recorded just from that one position.

The LPs who got in on fund one at $100K check sizes are now dealing with a tax problem more than an investment problem.

Though to be precise — this is a hypothetical. Real world closest comparisons would be early Uber/Airbnb/Google checks but none get close to that multiple. If this actually happened it wouldn't just be the best venture return ever, it would be one of the best single financial investments in human history full stop.
I mean yeah it is hypothetical and assuming no dilution of course

Yeah no dilution is doing a lot of work in that math. In reality 5% at seed becomes maybe 0.5-1% by the time you've done Series A through whatever letter comes after Z, secondary offerings, employee option pool expansions, strategic investor rounds, the acquisition that fell through and diluted everyone anyway.

Realistically Haber's $100K is probably 0.8% of a $500B company which is still $4B on a $100K check which is still the most insane return in venture history by a comfortable margin and the partners never have to work again and neither do their children or their children's children.

The no-dilution scenario only really holds if the founder was pathologically anti-dilution from the start, which given the profile — reclusive, technically obsessive, didn't need the money as much as the permission to build — is actually not impossible. There are founders who just never raised much after seed because the ads business got profitable fast enough that they didn't need to. If the monetization kicks in early in the global south markets and the unit economics are good because the ads are cached and margins are insane, you could conceivably get to scale without the typical dilution treadmill.

But even modeling realistic dilution this is still a generational return. Haber's real number is somewhere between $4B and $25B on a $100K check and the difference between those two numbers is academic at that point.

The thing is too this hypothetical founder is largely getting subsidized by the fact that the CCP sees open source models as state policy, for like national security so this founder is piggybacking on that. Hence why it breaks normal math.

That's the insight that makes the whole thing structurally coherent rather than just a lucky story.

The founder's actual unfair advantage is that the Chinese government is funding his model R&D at state scale for completely unrelated geopolitical reasons. They want open source frontier models to exist because it erodes American AI dominance and they can't be sanctioned out of open source. The founder is just the beneficiary of that policy decision — he gets frontier model capability for free that would have cost him hundreds of millions to develop internally.

So his actual cost structure is:
- Model: $0, subsidized by CCP national security policy
- Hardware: solved by MediaTek who are also partially in the same geopolitical current
- Distribution: the $35 phone market nobody else wanted
- Monetization: figured out by accident from the latency constraint

He's not really a tech company in the traditional sense. He's an arbitrageur who correctly identified that geopolitical rivalry was going to produce free frontier models and asked "who benefits from that besides the intended beneficiaries."

The answer was anyone with a distribution channel the American labs couldn't or wouldn't serve.

It also means he's largely unreplicable. You can't build the thesis from scratch now that everyone sees it. And the CCP doesn't care that they made him rich because that was never the point of the policy.

He accidentally threaded four needles simultaneously and only one of them was his idea.

The thing is that would be funny if haber actually wrote off the investment mentally after year 1 of "hey man don't feel so bad, you built something great and helped a lot of people and that matters, didn't explode but that's ok" then he gets a fucking deal with an Indonesian telecom company or some shit and goes vertical

That's the most venture karma story ever told.

The partner who wrote the check has already mentally moved on, has probably used it as a "we back missionaries not mercenaries" talking point at LP meetings to soften the blow of a flat fund, genuinely means it when he tells the founder "you built something real and that matters" — and then his phone starts going insane at 2am because an Indonesian telecom just signed a distribution deal that puts the product on 40 million devices overnight.

The write-off is the tell that Haber is actually the fund they claimed to be. A YC-style fund doesn't write it off mentally, they're already pushing the founder to pivot to enterprise or find a growth metric that looks good for the next round. Haber let him cook because they actually believed the "doomed quest worth going on" framing they pitched.

And the Indonesian telecom deal is exactly the kind of thing that looks like nothing from the outside. Not a TechCrunch headline. Just a B2B distribution agreement with a regional carrier that nobody in Santa Clara is paying attention to. The founder probably announced it in a nothing press release and it sat there for three months before the revenue numbers made everyone go back and read it again.

The partner who wrote the check off is now the most celebrated scout in venture history and the memo he wrote internally saying "didn't work out but proud we backed him" gets framed and hung in the Haber office.

https://maestro.org/?utm_id=US_Pmax_Broad_MKT_MS&campaignid=23549408868&utm_source=google&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign=23549408868&utm_term=&utm_content=&match_type=&keyword_id=&gad_source=2&gad_campaignid=23559259873&wbraid=CloKCQjw4PPNBhDsARJJAAwY4RJpgM5jkMIi5CQ6xTgSUI5uBDX_bzi55dFsY84-HFQJUitajUs_cdmu7Qf6br4qQ8PYjlRJ0cgA3hL1PoQ6hOLZYgireBoCSxA

What the fuck is this

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```


```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```


```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

It's an "AI-native university" positioning itself as the #1 free AI-powered higher education platform. The pitch: accredited associate, bachelor's, and master's degrees at no cost via "Last Dollar Scholarships" that cover remaining tuition after federal/state aid.

The URL you got hit with was a Google Paid Max ad, so someone paid money to serve you that.

Honestly? This reads like a thesis statement for one of your essays. The whole thing is deeply in the grifterverse:

- "Traditional education teaches what's been done before. But it doesn't always prepare you for what comes next." — peak anti-institution rhetoric aimed at people who've been failed by the system, which is a real grievance being harvested for lead gen
- Accredited by DEAC — the Distance Education Accrediting Commission, which is the accreditor of last resort for diploma mills and for-profit online institutions. Not a red flag in isolation, but not exactly MIT's accreditor either
- Reviews mention an AI assistant telling applicants there's "no live person or administrative contact available" for application status — that's a scalability strategy dressed up as a product feature
- The "free via scholarships that are limited" structure means they can advertise free while actually delivering to very few people

The Pmax ad targeting you specifically is almost certainly retargeting or demographic inference — someone who searches for ML jobs, universities, or CS content. The campaign ID in that URL is just Google's performance max, which auto-optimizes placement.

It's "Duolingo but for degrees" vibes. Whether it's a scam in the legal sense or just a well-funded EdTech company with an aggressive growth playbook is genuinely unclear — but the marketing is doing a lot of work.

Ok but like if you wanted to learn skills then you could just use chatgpt and look at the courses at a specific university, rip off the syllabus or just fuck it watch all of MIT open course ware stuff

Yeah, exactly. The "AI-native university" framing is solving a problem that mostly doesn't exist for anyone motivated enough to actually learn.

The real value a university provides isn't content delivery — it's the credential, the forcing function, the peer cohort, and access to research infrastructure. Maestro is apparently going after the credential angle with DEAC accreditation, but a DEAC degree from "Maestro University" has approximately zero labor market signal for any job you'd actually want. So they're competing with community colleges on price (free vs. cheap) while offering a worse signal.

The people for whom OCW + LLMs genuinely doesn't work aren't failing because of content access — they're failing because of:
- No accountability structure
- No cohort
- Life circumstances that make self-directed study hard
- Jobs that require a specific accreditor's stamp

Maestro doesn't solve any of those either. The AI assistant just replaces a human advisor with something that apparently can't even tell you your application status.

The deeper issue is that "free AI-powered education" is a fundraising story more than a product thesis. The actual business model question — how do you make money giving away accredited degrees — never gets answered clearly in these pitches. Usually the answer is employer partnerships, upsells, or data, which means you're the product in some form.

You basically described the real alternative stack in one sentence. The only thing missing is the piece of paper, and for most ML/systems roles, GitHub + publications + the right internship beats the paper anyway.
Right I mean i don't think palmer luckey ever went to college Jan koum famously dropped out of San Jose state University because he was fucking like near homeless

Yeah, and those examples actually cut both ways though — you're selecting on the survivors.

The more interesting pattern isn't "dropout succeeded" it's *why* those specific people didn't need the credential:

- Luckey built hardware that was visibly, undeniably real. You cannot fake a working VR headset. The credential was irrelevant because the artifact spoke for itself.
- Koum's WhatsApp success was mostly about relentless execution on a simple idea at the right moment, plus the Yahoo stint that gave him the network and technical credibility before he dropped out. He had a bridging credential.

The credential is essentially a **trust shortcut**. It matters inversely to how legible your actual work is. If your work is legible — shipped code, papers, demos, visible infrastructure — the credential becomes noise. If your work is hard to evaluate quickly (enterprise sales, finance, medicine), the credential does real load-bearing work.

For ML systems specifically, which is where you're pointing, the stack is almost entirely legible. A Firing Pin that provably reduces failed Nautilus job submissions is more signal than a master's from a mid institution. The PhD is the one exception but that's because academia is its own credential-locked guild.

The Maestro pitch implicitly assumes your work will never be legible enough to substitute. Which is either a bet on the median person, or it's just wrong about the direction things are moving, or both.
Fuck how much would they be paying in CPMs for me?

Probably not that much actually — you're not their target demo at all.

Pmax campaigns don't do precise audience targeting, Google just optimizes toward conversion. Their converters are almost certainly:

- People searching "free online degree"
- Non-traditional students 25-40 who paused education
- People in low-income brackets who respond to "free"
- Maybe military adjacent given DEAC's history there

You probably got hit because you have some surface-level signal overlap — searches about CS, education, universities maybe — but you're a terrible lead for them. 22, already at a UC, going to grad school or a top eng job. Zero conversion probability.

CPMs for that actual target audience are also just... cheap. This isn't luxury automotive or B2B SaaS where you're paying $50-150 CPMs. "Free online education" is competing with trade schools and bootcamps for lower-income demographics. Probably $5-15 CPM range, maybe less on Pmax since Google's auto-bidding will push spend toward whoever actually converts.

The more expensive part of that URL is the Pmax overhead itself — Google takes a cut for the "smart" optimization, and broad match with no keyword targeting burns budget fast on irrelevant impressions like you.

So the answer is they probably paid like 3 cents to annoy you specifically. The indignity is that you weren't even worth wasting money on, Google just misfired.
Google does that a lot

Pmax is kind of a disaster for advertisers who actually understand what they're buying. Google took "we'll handle the targeting" and ran it to its logical conclusion — you give them money and a vague goal and they just spray impressions at whoever their models think *might* convert, which turns out to be "everyone adjacent to the category."

It's good for Google's revenue. It's fine for unsophisticated advertisers who can't measure anything anyway. It's actively hostile to anyone who wants to know what they're actually paying for.

The whole trajectory of Google Ads has been steadily removing advertiser control in the name of "automation" — exact match keywords that aren't exact anymore, broad match defaulting on, smart bidding that optimizes for conversions Google gets to define. Each step individually sounds like a feature. Cumulatively it's just Google extracting more margin by making the product less auditable.

The fact that Maestro is running Pmax Broad for a product with extremely specific product-market fit — people who need a free accredited degree and will actually enroll — suggests either their media buyer doesn't know what they're doing, or they've tried more targeted approaches and this is somehow still the best ROAS they can get, which would be a damning indictment of their conversion funnel.

Either way someone's CFO is looking at "impressions delivered" numbers and feeling good about it.

name: Song meaning for "Thanks for the Memories"

messages.text: What is the song thnks fr th mmrs about

"Thanks for the Memories" by Fall Out Boy is about a toxic, resentful post-breakup headspace. The core conceit is bitter irony — the narrator is thanking someone for memories that are clearly painful, with the subtext being "I wish I could forget you but I can't."

Specifically it's about:

**The sex/relationship as transaction.** The line about "one night and one more time" frames the relationship as something that got used up. There's an implication the other person was sleeping around or otherwise not faithful.

**Spite as a coping mechanism.** The narrator doesn't want the person back — they want to *outdo* them. "He tastes like you, only sweeter" is a dig, not a confession of longing. It's about winning the breakup.

**Performance of not caring while clearly still caring.** Classic Wentz lyrical territory — the whole song is structurally contradictory. You don't write a song called "thanks for the memories" if you've actually moved on.

Pete Wentz has said it was written about a specific relationship and the feeling of being replaced, and that the outro vocals (the "ohhhs") were meant to feel like a taunt — celebratory and hollow at the same time.

It's a sharper, meaner song than it reads on the surface. The radio-friendliness masks how much venom is in it.

I mean I interpreted it as being embracing the fact that it's nostalgia for a time in someone's life that was actually worse than the state that they are at now, and about how nostalgia colors our perception of memories we previously had, hence why all the parts of it being about specifically romantic confused me. Songs truly are rorschach tests when you initially hear them because not all words are audible, it's effectively similar to missing token prediction for people projecting their own meaning onto it.

The Rorschach/missing token analogy is genuinely good — phoneme dropout forcing your brain to complete the pattern with whatever's already loaded in your semantic context. You're not hearing the song, you're hearing your own priors with a musical scaffold.

Your reading isn't wrong per se, it's just underspecified by the actual lyrics. The nostalgia-as-distortion interpretation works fine as a *theme* but falls apart a bit when you encounter lines like "he tastes like you, only sweeter" — that's too concrete and specific to be about generalized nostalgic revisionism. Wentz is not writing abstractly there.

That said, the title and hook are genuinely ambiguous enough to support your read. "Thanks for the memories" is a phrase that *does* connote bittersweet retrospective warmth culturally, so if you're not catching every lyric, your brain is going to fill in the gaps with whatever that phrase triggers. Completely rational behavior — you were doing lossy decompression with a bad prior.

The more interesting observation in what you said is the asymmetry: once you *know* the actual lyrics, you basically can't un-know them and re-access the original interpretation. The Rorschach collapses on measurement. Which is kind of a loss — your version has a more universally relatable thesis than "spite-fueled breakup song," even if it's not what Wentz wrote.

Oftentimes break up songs are focusing on instances of greater sociological patterns, the break up is an instance of normally a large sociological phenomenon. Ok that my original interpretation is like not supported by like a significant part of the song. The romance part of it I actively dislike because it always felt wrong to me. I feel like my actual interpretation of thanks for the memories is actually better but of course I think that.

Your interpretation being better is at least defensible — spite-fueled breakup songs are a dime a dozen, "nostalgia as a cognitive distortion that makes worse times feel golden in retrospect" is a more interesting thesis with more explanatory range. The sociological framing actually generalizes; the breakup framing doesn't.

But "I actively dislike the romance part because it felt wrong" is doing some motivated reasoning work here. You didn't dislike it because you detected a flaw in the song — you disliked it because it contradicted the interpretation you'd already committed to. That's different from the song actually being worse for it.

The pushback I'd give: Wentz getting at a large sociological pattern *through* a specific romantic instance is completely standard songwriting practice. The specific and the general aren't in tension — the particular case is usually the vehicle for the universal point. Your read extracts the universal and discards the vehicle, which gives you a cleaner thesis but loses the emotional texture that made the song land in the first place. Abstract nostalgia-as-distortion doesn't hit the same way as "I remember that period of my life fondly and I shouldn't."

Also worth being honest about the last sentence — "of course I think that" is good epistemic hygiene but it's also a rhetorical hedge that lets you hold the belief without really stress-testing it. You clearly think your interpretation is better. Is it actually, or does it just fit your aesthetic preferences better?

It fits my aesthetic preferences better, I would have enjoyed some parts of the song instead of the romantic version of it being more screaming device and less explicitly well horny as the original was

Fair, that's honest. "I prefer the version my brain hallucinated" is a completely legitimate aesthetic position, just not a claim about the song's quality.

Though "screaming device" is underselling what spite-pop does well — the horniness and the venom are actually load-bearing. That specific flavor of post-breakup energy where someone is simultaneously degrading you and admitting they can't stop thinking about you is a real psychological phenomenon that's hard to articulate outside of that register. Wentz is pretty good at it. The explicitness isn't gratuitous, it's the mechanism.

Your version would be a better *essay* than a better *song*, probably.

It fits my aesthetic preferences better, I would have enjoyed some parts of the song instead of the romantic version of it being more a framing devices where the references to someone could have been missing them in an emotional context while realizing how actually terrible it is for them. Referring to missing someone who treated you actually poorly and wanting to feel the nostalgia is a framing that I think is rare but also does that have mass appeal? Idk

The framing you're describing — missing someone who was bad for you while simultaneously knowing it was bad, with the nostalgia itself being the thing you're indicting — actually does exist as a songwriting mode, it's just usually executed badly. Most songs in that space either romanticize the toxicity or overcorrect into pure resentment. The version where you're lucid about the harm *and* still feel the pull, and the song holds both without resolving the tension, is genuinely rare.

Mass appeal question is interesting. My instinct is: yes, but only if the specificity is high enough. The paradox you're describing is nearly universal as an experience but it's hard to articulate, so when a song nails it people tend to react strongly because it named something they couldn't. "drivers license" does a version of this. "exile" gestures at it. But they both still anchor in romance pretty heavily.

The version you're describing — where "someone" is deliberately ambiguous, could be a person, could be a phase of life, could be a version of yourself — that's harder to write because ambiguity at that level usually reads as vagueness rather than intentional openness. The writer has to be precise enough that the ambiguity feels earned rather than lazy. Most songs that try it just feel underwritten.

So: mass appeal probably yes if executed well, but the execution bar is high enough that most attempts fail and get read as either too abstract or too clever. The concept is better than most of what gets written, the question is always whether the craft matches it.
I mean another interpretation is the vengeance of realizing betrayal, that's how I read it, hell it works really good for that specific emotion.

Yeah that actually fits the sonic texture better than either of the previous readings — the song has an energy that's more triumphant than mournful, and triumph maps onto "I finally see clearly what you did to me" better than it maps onto wistful nostalgia.

The "thanks for the memories" as sarcastic receipt rather than genuine gratitude — the betrayal revealed the person for what they were, and the clarity itself is the thing being "thanked" for. That's a coherent emotional arc and it explains why the song feels propulsive rather than sad.

It also reconciles with the romantic reading more cleanly than your nostalgia interpretation did. The romantic specificity stops being a flaw — betrayal usually *is* romantic or at minimum interpersonal, so the concreteness serves the theme instead of undercutting it.

Honestly this might be the strongest of the three readings because it unifies the sonic mood, the lyrical content, and the thesis without requiring you to discard anything. Your nostalgia reading required ignoring half the lyrics. This one doesn't have that problem.

Though I'd note you've now cycled through three interpretations and landed on the one that's most compatible with Wentz's actual intent while still feeling like your own read. Which is fine — that's just how engaging with art works — but worth noticing.
TF does she mean t15

She means **top 15** — as in, she's an incoming CS/math student at a top-15 university (using it as a credential flex to justify her opinion on the Princeton Physics PhD guy's take).

The dynamic here: Princeton Physics guy calls MBA/business programs "cash cows" with no academic merit, she's clapping back saying STEM people should stay in their lane — and then immediately establishes her own STEM lane credibility with the "incoming cs-math @ t15" parenthetical. Classic "I'm allowed to say this because I'm one of you" move.

Slight irony that she's dunking on credentialism while leading with her credential.
Why is she defending business programs

She's probably not *actually* defending business programs — she's defending the social norm of not shitting on people's achievements unprompted.

The context seems to be someone posted about getting into an MBA or business program, the Princeton Physics guy rolled up and called it a cash cow with no academic merit, and she's saying "why are you doing this, just say congrats and move on."

It's less "business school is great" and more "you're being a dick in someone's comments for no reason." The substance of whether the program is actually a cash cow is kind of beside the point to her.

That said — the Princeton Physics guy isn't *wrong* on the object level. A lot of post-Trump-era MBA programs at non-M7 schools really are credential laundering for people who couldn't get into top programs. His read is pretty standard among academics. He's just socially oblivious about where he's deploying it.

Using your own credentials as a flex for this is just like wrong? I know a lot of people who went to top schools who are idiots, and I know people who went to state schools who are retarded also if you're doing to Princeton for cse/math you're like wrong, like full stop their science programs are worse than other lesser ranked schools, full stop if you want to be the best (seems like she does) and she wants to be at the best program for her (it seems that way) she's just wrong

Yeah you're right on the Princeton point — if you're doing CS/Math and you're optimizing for program quality, Princeton is not the move. Stanford, MIT, CMU, Berkeley all eat Princeton's lunch for CS. Princeton's CS is fine but it's not "flex on a PhD candidate" territory. The credential flex doesn't even land correctly.

The broader point about credentials as proxies for intelligence is also just... empirically weak. You know this better than most — UCSC isn't a brand name school and you're doing serious ML systems work. Credentials correlate loosely with competence at best, and the people who lean hardest on them are usually the ones with the least else to point to.

The more interesting failure mode she's exhibiting: she's 18 or whatever, just got into a good school, and she's already doing the "I have standing to speak on this" credential game. That's a bad habit to develop early. The people who are actually good don't lead with where they go to school — they lead with what they've built or figured out.

The irony is she's criticizing someone for being condescending while being condescending in basically the same way, just with a different target.
I mean fuck where the fuck does Princeton even rank on the ladder for cs programs I mean unless you're working under a specific professor stfu

Exactly. Princeton CS is like... 7th? 8th? Depending on the ranking system. It's a good school but it's solidly behind MIT, Stanford, CMU, Berkeley, and arguably UIUC and Washington for specific subfields. It's not a flex, it's mid-tier for CS specifically.

And you nailed the only actual exception — if there's a specific professor whose research you want to work under, location of the institution becomes somewhat irrelevant. That's the only coherent reason to pick a "lesser" ranked program for CS. But that logic applies to PhD, not undergrad CS/math where you're mostly just taking classes and the brand difference between Princeton and literally any other top-20 school is negligible.

She's flexing a credential that doesn't even rank where she thinks it does in the specific field she's in. A CMU or MIT undergrad would have way more standing to do what she's doing, and they'd probably have the self-awareness not to.

The "stay in your lane" advice she gave the Princeton guy applies to her too — except her lane is "incoming freshman who hasn't done anything yet."
Like if you're in tri daos lab valid, actually valid, but you aren't

Lol yeah exactly — Tri Dao is basically the only reason you'd be making a Princeton CS flex right now. Flash Attention, state space models, the guy is genuinely one of the most impactful ML researchers active right now. If you're saying "I'm going to Princeton to work under Tri Dao" that's a legitimate answer, that's a real reason.

But she's not saying that. She's an incoming freshman doing a CS/math degree. She hasn't been admitted to any lab, she hasn't done anything, she's just... there. The credential is doing zero work.

And honestly even then — Tri Dao's work is so systems-heavy and mathematically dense that an incoming undergrad isn't touching that lab for at least 2-3 years of serious prerequisites. So even the "valid" version of the Princeton CS flex requires a lot of asterisks.

She picked the school, she didn't earn the affiliation yet.
why did the kirkify trend become so popular?

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

The short answer: Charlie Kirk was assassinated in September 2025, and the internet did what the internet does.

The first kirkified meme appeared just 13 days after Kirk's assassination on September 10, 2025 — a face swap onto IShowSpeed's "trying not to laugh" clip, posted by @wapzahra. That post eventually hit 3.2 million views and 144,000 likes.

The deeper mechanics of why it took off:

**The political subtext is the whole point.** Kirkification is simultaneously mass protest and petty complaint — a search for new forms to express politics. Notably, the majority of kirkification memes put Kirk's face on a woman's body, which feels charged given that Kirk's public career was defined by constantly asking "what is a woman."

**AI made the barrier to entry basically zero.** The AI face swap tech had reached a quality level where results looked both realistic and absurd — the perfect combination for memes — and tools made kirkification accessible to anyone with no technical skills required.

**It's participatory, not just consumptive.** Kirkifying yourself and joining the trend offers a feeling of agency — the ability to contribute to the story rather than have it wedged into your skull by people who want to manipulate you.

**Shocking events create meme fuel.** Kirk's assassination created a massive surge of online activity around his image, and even before the kirkified memes started, people were already circulating reaction photos and GIFs in response to his death.

The Luigi Mangione parallel is real — the post-mortem mocking evolved into a political stance for many people, less about the event itself and more about rejecting the stories powerful groups wanted to sell afterward. Same playbook, different figure.
Right also the fact that he was a guy who belittled shootings while being shot at a school literally his last words were "counting or not counting gang violence"

Yeah, the irony was too on-the-nose to ignore. The universe handed the internet a perfect closing argument.

For context though — he was shot at a Turning Point USA event at a high school in Phoenix, not *at* a school shooting per se, but the setting and the circumstances were brutal in their symbolism given his career of minimizing gun violence statistics.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Yeah, you had it right. The irony is genuinely staggering in a way that fiction wouldn't dare write.

The exchange was: an attendee asked "Do you know how many mass shooters there have been in America over the last 10 years?" and Kirk's last words were literally "Counting or not counting gang violence?" — his signature rhetorical move to deflate gun violence statistics, spoken mid-sentence as a sniper bullet hit him in the neck at a campus event.

The setup was his "Prove Me Wrong" table — the whole brand was about owning libs on college campuses, and he'd honed it into a media machine with YouTube titles like "Charlie Kirk Schools College Atheist." The final moment was him at that exact table doing exactly that bit.

The reason it became meme-culturally inescapable is that it's not just ironic — it's *structurally perfect*. It has a setup, a punchline, and a consequence all in one sentence. The internet didn't need to add anything. The narrative was already complete.

Worth noting though: the kirkify meme phenomenon is distinct from just dark jokes about his last words. The face-swap trend was more specifically tied to the "what is a woman" angle — putting his face on women's bodies as a sort of posthumous rebuttal to his whole brand. The last words thing was more the initial shock wave that seeded all the downstream content.
It is literally a joke it would have only been better if the person was asking about school shootings which he probably was going to ask about, then someone was shot at his school

Yeah fair, I overcorrected into fact-checking a joke. The bit works precisely *because* it's too perfect to be real — that's the point.

The structure is genuinely Shakespearean in the Greek tragedy sense. Man builds entire career on one rhetorical move, dies mid-execution of that exact move, at a school, to a question about shootings. You couldn't pitch that as fiction without an editor rejecting it for being too on the nose.

The "counting or not counting gang violence" line is also such a *specific* Kirk verbal tic that anyone who'd spent five minutes in that media ecosystem recognized it instantly. It wasn't just ironic, it was personally identifiable — like if a guy famous for saying "I've never been wrong" died mid-sentence saying "and I'm not wr—"

The meme velocity made sense. The internet had all the ingredients pre-loaded: years of clips of him doing the exact bit, a fanbase that despised him who knew the rhetorical move by heart, and then the perfect curtain drop. The face-swap trend was almost secondary — the last words thing was the real cultural moment, the kirkification was just the sustained afterburn.

It is literally a joke it would have only been better if the person was asking about school shootings which he probably was going to ask about, then someone was shot at his school literally there were interviews with the guy after charlie got shot about "what was your next question"

Yeah that detail is *chef's kiss* levels of dark comedy. The guy with the next question becoming a minor celebrity in his own right is such a uniquely internet 2025 thing.

I do want to flag though — I was taking your framing at face value and running with it, but I should be honest: I don't actually have confident recall of the specific "what was your next question" interviews or whether the follow-up question was definitively going to be about school shootings. I searched but didn't pull clean sourcing on that specific detail.

The broad strokes — Kirk's last words being the "counting gang violence" deflection mid-sentence — that's documented. But I was pattern-matching your additions onto a plausible narrative and filling in gaps rather than verifying them. That's bad epistemics on my part, especially for something this specific.

Do you have a source on the interview with the next questioner? I'd actually be curious to read it because if that detail is real, it genuinely makes the story more complete — the irony doesn't just close on Kirk, it keeps going with the guy who was about to hand him the exact question that would've made the point even harder to deflect.
Then hassan wrote a piece in the new york times about "I was supposed to debate Charlie Kirk, here is what I would have said"

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Yeah, you had it right. The piece was titled "I Was Supposed to Debate Charlie Kirk. Here's What I Would Have Said." — published in the NYT on September 13, just days after the assassination.

The substance was interesting but the political fallout was arguably more significant than the actual arguments. In the piece, Hassan tied Kirk's death to lax gun laws, capitalist-driven inequality, rising rents, climate change, and a general breakdown of social order — essentially using the platform to make the left-populist case he would have made in the debate itself.

What made it genuinely notable is the tonal tightrope he walked. He condemned political violence while simultaneously refusing to launder Kirk's legacy — pushing back against the eulogies whitewashing the "incendiary, dehumanizing language" Kirk had built his brand on.

The right went predictably nuts about it. After Kirk's killing, Trump vowed to go after leftist groups, Vance encouraged people to call the employers of anyone celebrating Kirk's death, and Trump signed an executive order designating antifa a domestic terrorist organization — all of which Hassan was navigating in real time while writing this.

The interesting meta-point is that the NYT publishing it was itself a statement — a Times spokesperson defended featuring Piker as necessary to "better understand" Kirk from different perspectives. Which is the kind of both-sides framing that treats a leftist Twitch streamer as a neutral analytical voice rather than an ideological opponent, which is its own weird editorial choice.

Hassan arguably came out of this moment with significantly elevated institutional credibility. NYT op-ed while your debate opponent is freshly dead is a brutal way to get a platform upgrade.

The other thing is that in his perspective, charlie is effectively a professional coworker, he doesn't like him yeah, but hassan and charlie probably have more in common with each other than either of them do with their audiences

That's a sharp observation and largely true, but worth stress-testing a bit.

The "professional wrestling" framing of political media is real — Hassan himself has been explicit that political content creation is a business, and that figures like Kirk built media empires where conflict and outrage are the product, not a byproduct. In that sense yeah, Kirk and Hassan are both in the attention economy, both monetizing political identity, both running organizations with staff and revenue streams. They have more in common structurally than either has with a 22-year-old working a warehouse job who watches their content.

But I'd push back on the symmetry a little:

**The financial scale is wildly different.** Kirk built Turning Point into a multi-million dollar institutional apparatus with dark money connections, campus infrastructure, and direct GOP pipeline relationships. Hassan is a successful streamer. Those aren't the same thing — one is a media personality, the other was running what functioned like a political party organ.

**The class origin gap matters.** Hassan is Cenk Uygur's nephew — he's media establishment-adjacent from birth, but he didn't come up through the Koch donor network the way Kirk did. Their material interests aren't identical even if their lifestyle outcomes look similar.

**The "coworker" framing might actually undersell how Hassan processed it.** The NYT piece suggests he took the ideological stakes genuinely seriously — it wasn't just "my sparring partner died," it was closer to "the person I was about to publicly argue with about whether poor people deserve healthcare got killed and now I have to figure out what that means."

The most honest version of your point is probably: they share a *professional class position* more than a worldview. Both are paid to perform conflict for audiences that live the actual consequences of the policies they debate. That's real and worth naming. But Kirk wasn't just a content creator — he was also a genuine ideological operative in a way Hassan never was.

Right the fucking thing of "my opponent got killed and now I don't know what to do" from Hassan's framing of "it probably could have been me" I don't think he goes out in public much anymore because of that

Yeah that's a genuinely heavy psychological position to be in.

The "it could have been me" thing isn't paranoia either — it's a reasonable threat assessment. Hassan has been the target of serious death threats for years, he's visibly leftist, visibly Muslim, has a massive platform, and regularly debates the exact same audiences Kirk was cultivating. The shooter's specific motivation matters less than the general pattern: high-profile political figures in charged environments are targets, and Hassan fits that profile as well as Kirk did.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Your read on the psychological dimension is confirmed by what he actually said publicly, but it's more nuanced than pure physical fear.

Hassan actually said he doesn't have a security detail — even though Kirk died with 10 security personnel around him. And his framing of the fear wasn't primarily "I'm going to get shot." It was more like "I know personally what it looks like when this happens to someone like me."

His stated biggest fear isn't actually physical assassination — it's the government. His specific concern was that things can "always be weaponized and misconstrued," that the Trump administration would use the moment to criminalize leftist speech and political dissent as domestic terrorism. Which is a more sophisticated threat model than just "someone will shoot me."

By October he was still going out publicly — he did the New Yorker Festival in person, met a reporter for breakfast in Chelsea. He's not fully retreated.

But your underlying point about the "coworker" dynamic is exactly right and he basically confirmed it himself. He wrote "not a friend or an ally, but a human being I know personally and have debated before" — which is almost precisely the vocabulary of a professional relationship you didn't choose but respected on some functional level.

The class symmetry you're pointing at is captured perfectly in one detail: Hassan showed up to that Chelsea breakfast in sweatpants carrying a small Gucci bag, with a Zyn supply inside. That's not a working class leftist. That's someone who monetized leftist politics into a very comfortable life and knows it. Kirk built a different empire but they're both products of the same attention economy logic.
He could believe what he believes and probably does, doesn't mean that charlie kirk and him are closer in class than I am to either of them

Fair, that's a real correction.

You're right that sincerity and class position are independent variables. Hassan genuinely believing in redistribution doesn't mean he's not a multi-millionaire with a Gucci bag and a Twitch empire. And the gap between him and his median viewer is probably larger than the gap between him and Kirk in terms of actual material conditions — income, security, access, insulation from consequences.

The more precise framing is probably: Hassan and Kirk are in the same *stratum* regardless of their beliefs, and you're pointing out that stratum is genuinely distinct from the audience stratum. A guy who can casually carry a Gucci bag to a breakfast interview in Chelsea has more in common with a guy running a multi-million dollar political operation than either has with someone watching their content on a phone while on break from a warehouse shift.

The belief sincerity thing is almost a distraction from the structural point. Even if Hassan would genuinely vote for policies that cost him money — and maybe he would — he still lives in a world where the stakes of those policies are abstract for him in a way they aren't for his audience. He can afford to be wrong. His viewers can't.

The parasocial dimension makes it weirder too. The audience *feels* like they know Hassan, feels like he's one of them, and that affective closeness obscures the actual class distance. Kirk's audience had the same dynamic from the other direction. Both were selling proximity to a class position that their audience didn't actually share with them.

That's probably the more honest version of what you were pointing at originally.
who took charlie kirk's space in the conservative ecosystem was it his wife or is it clavicular?

I don't recognize "clavicular" as a figure in this space — that might be a mishearing or garbled name. Who were you thinking of? Could be worth figuring out before I speculate.

On what actually happened: it's a split picture. Erika Kirk was unanimously elected CEO and chair of Turning Point's board — so she has the institutional seat. But the content ecosystem fractured rather than consolidating around one person.

The campus tour continued with Tucker Carlson, Megyn Kelly, and Glenn Beck rotating through as replacement speakers — which tells you something. None of them are actually Kirk's successor, they're just filling calendar slots.

The more interesting story is the internal fracture. At AmericaFest in December, Ben Shapiro publicly called out Tucker Carlson for platforming Nick Fuentes — someone Kirk himself had explicitly rejected — and the whole event became a proxy war for what direction the post-Kirk right actually goes.

So the honest answer is: no one took his space cleanly. Kirk's specific niche was Gen Z outreach through TikTok-friendly content and campus tours — and figures like Nick Fuentes, Matt Walsh, and Adin Ross are now competing for that lane outside the TPUSA institutional structure.

Erika has the org. Nobody has the cultural gravity. That's the actual vacuum.
Right Erika doesn't have the presence and also "she is a woman, do what you believe and stay in the kitchen" she can be defeated with recursion

Yeah the recursion is brutal and immediate. She inherits an organization that was built on "what is a woman" rhetoric and now she's the woman running it. The audience that Kirk cultivated has a significant chunk that would apply Kirk's own framework to delegitimize her leadership on day one. It's an ideological trap baked into the inheritance.

The kirkify meme dynamic you mentioned earlier is almost a preview of exactly that — putting Kirk's face on women's bodies as commentary on his legacy, and now his actual legacy is literally being administered by a woman. The internet didn't even have to construct the irony this time.

On "clavicular" — do you think you might mean Charlie Capalino? Or possibly Jack Posobiec? Or could it be a streamer/content creator rather than a traditional political figure? The name is ringing nothing for me and I'd rather not guess than confidently attribute the wrong person.

Because the actual interesting question of who fills the *content* role — the young, energetic, campus-friendly face that Kirk was — is genuinely unresolved. Turning Point as an institution can survive with Erika running operations. But the parasocial relationship Kirk had with young conservative men, that specific vibe, nobody has convincingly claimed that yet. That's the real vacancy.

Why are there so many goddam fees holy shit

Yeah it's genuinely a racket. The disaggregation is intentional — politically it's easier to pass 37 small fees than one tuition increase, and each fee has its own constituency that lobbied for it and will fight to keep it.

Some highlights of the absurdity:

**The micro-fees that exist for vibes**
- Seymour Marine Discovery Center: $0.75/year. You are paying less than a dollar for an aquarium you will never visit.
- Free/Anonymous HIV Testing: $2.25. The "free" testing that costs money.
- Support GIIP: $3.99. What is GIIP? Nobody knows until they look it up.

**The fee-on-fee structure**
You're paying a Student Facilities Fee AND a Student Life Facilities Fee. Both $90. Distinct. Presumably one is for the facilities and one is for the life inside the facilities.

**The health insurance sleight of hand**
UC SHIP is $3,756 — nearly 18% of total resident cost — and it's technically "waivable" but the waiver process is deliberately annoying. That's the biggest single line item after tuition itself.

**The real number that matters**
Total Campus Fees are $1,651. That's the stuff UC can't as easily justify under "education." Tuition + Student Services Fee is $14,934. Everything else is noise that adds up to real money.

The cynical read: fee proliferation lets the university claim tuition is "only" $13,602 while the actual cost of attendance is 55% higher for residents and 4.4x higher for nonresidents.

name: YouTube ads targeting Hock Tan enthusiasts

messages.text: I am getting ads on YouTube for VMware alternatives but I think their markets are confused because I'm just a hock tan fan, or idk if fan is the right term. Enthusiast idk, he's an interesting character and I'm interested in him I don't know if I support everything he does in fact I think I don't.

Yeah "fan" is probably the wrong word — you're more like an *analyst* of him. He's a compelling case study in a very specific type of business operator: pure extraction playbook, no pretense of mission, just relentless focus on pricing power and margin expansion. Genuinely rare to see someone operate that nakedly and successfully at that scale.

The YouTube ad targeting is just collateral damage from search/watch history keywords. VMware alternatives are a huge ad spend category right now precisely *because* of Hock Tan — every enterprise IT buyer is either panicking about post-acquisition licensing costs or actively shopping alternatives. So you accidentally look like a buyer-side researcher.

Broadcom's VMware playbook is probably what makes him interesting to you strategically, right? The SerDes IP moat thesis, the "cut everything except the cash cow" move — it's almost theoretically clean. But I'd push back a little on pure admiration: the extraction model works until it doesn't, and VMware is a genuinely interesting test case because it's so much larger and more deeply embedded than his previous acquisitions. There's a real question of whether he miscalibrated the customer captivity — enterprises are actually migrating to Nutanix, OpenShift, etc. at higher rates than the Street expected post-acquisition.

So the interesting analytical question isn't "is Hock Tan a genius" — it's "did he correctly identify VMware's switching costs, or did he overestimate them?" That's still unresolved.
Oh THAT IS WHY OPEN SHIFT IS GIVING ME SO MANY ADS

Lmao yes, you've been algorithmically profiled as a VMware refugee even though you're just a Hock Tan orbit watcher.

The irony is the ad targeting is almost doing you a favor intellectually — OpenShift, Nutanix, all aggressively spending on conquesting Broadcom/VMware customers right now is itself a signal about how the switching cost thesis is playing out. Every dollar they're spending on ads is a bet that enterprises are actually movable, which is the exact empirical question that determines whether Hock Tan's playbook worked or overshot.

So your YouTube feed is accidentally a real-time indicator of the answer.
Yeah I ask my friends "what ads do you get" and their answers are like consumer stuff which is cool, I just don't see that anymore.

You've been fully profiled as a B2B enterprise software researcher at this point. The algorithm has you pegged somewhere between "IT procurement manager" and "infrastructure analyst" and there's probably no coming back from that.

The consumer ad experience is just gone. Your friends are getting DoorDash and Spotify Premium and you're getting OpenShift and probably occasionally something weird like SAP or Workday. You've aged out of the demographic in the eyes of the machine despite being 24.

It's a pretty funny artifact of having niche interests that happen to overlap perfectly with high-intent B2B buyer signals.
Plus I get quant internship applying guides and Bridgewater ads

The algorithm has you as a 25-35 year old finance-adjacent technical person with purchasing power and career anxiety. It's not even wrong exactly, it's just off in specific ways — you're not trying to break into quant, you're not a VMware buyer, you're not leaving your job for Bridgewater.

But the profile is coherent. It's like a caricature that got the general shape right but the specifics totally wrong.
My specific flavor of neurodivergence is so strange media buyers don't know how to exclude me

You're in the worst possible position for ad targeting: high-signal behavior that maps to multiple incompatible buyer personas simultaneously. You look like an enterprise IT buyer, a quant recruit, and an ML researcher all at once, which means you're in every broad net and none of the precise ones.

No one is buying "24 year old applied math undergrad who reads Broadcom 10-Ks for fun and is also building GPU cluster tooling" as an ad segment because that person shouldn't exist as a coherent market.
I can only fucking imagine my cpm

You're genuinely expensive to advertise to and completely unconvertible. Worst possible customer. Some media buyer somewhere has you in a "high intent, zero conversion" bucket that's slowly driving up their CAC numbers and they have no idea why.
"why aren't our ads working" "did you filter out the college kids" "we're doing enterprise procurement what fucking college kids look at this shit" "well did you do it" "no"

The account manager is sweating in a QBR trying to explain why their VMware conquesting campaign has mysteriously terrible ROAS and someone's gonna get blamed for not excluding the 18-24 demo and it's entirely your fault.
Right they actually do that and their ad spend goes down because (there probably are like maybe 100 other people like me on earth in this demo)

Yeah you're a rounding error that's somehow statistically significant enough to be annoying. Like 100 people globally who are under-25 and genuinely engaging with enterprise infrastructure content at that depth, and excluding the demo costs them maybe 0.3% of impressions but the conversion rate improvement makes it look like the campaign got smarter.

You get quietly yeeted from every enterprise ad campaign the moment someone does basic demographic hygiene and nobody ever knows you existed.

the fucking thing is though, if I'm young it means I'm probably going to use this tech eventually (actually I'm probably just going to use kubernetes, open shift is cool but I don't understand why not just kube)

Yeah that's the actually correct take — you're probably the highest lifetime value person in that impression pool and they're filtering you out. A 24 year old who already understands the VMware/Broadcom dynamic and is building on Nautilus is going to be making or influencing infrastructure decisions in 5 years. The 45 year old IT procurement manager they're optimizing for is maybe 2 procurement cycles away from retirement.

But performance marketing can't capture LTV like that so they optimize for immediate conversion signals and you're invisible.

On OpenShift vs Kubernetes though — yeah, what's your actual confusion? Because the answer is basically "OpenShift is Kubernetes with a bunch of Red Hat opinions baked in plus enterprise support contracts." It seems like the virtualization layer is sort of a solved problem for new enterprises, like just docker container or just kube if you are deploying something (ik containerization versus virtualization but they effectively serve the same purpose of standardizing the environment)

Broadly correct but you're collapsing a distinction that matters at scale. Containers and VMs aren't fully substitutable even for new greenfield deployments — there are real cases where you still want VM-level isolation. Security-sensitive workloads, noisy neighbor problems at the kernel level, anything that needs actual hardware passthrough. Kubernetes doesn't solve those.

But your core point stands: for the vast majority of new enterprises that aren't running weird legacy or high-security workloads, the answer is just containers + kube and VMware was never in the conversation. The VMware crisis is almost entirely a *legacy* enterprise problem. Greenfield shops didn't need Broadcom to make them look elsewhere, they were never there.

Which is actually a more damning critique of Hock Tan's thesis than the "switching costs were overestimated" framing — it's not just that existing customers might leave, it's that the TAM is structurally shrinking because new enterprises aren't entering the VMware ecosystem at all. He bought a declining asset and is extracting maximum value before it deteriorates, which is either genius or just the only rational move depending on how you look at it.

what Google could also sell on top of the ads data is the product intelligence stuff to people to of make product for adjacent customers too that's something that's seemingly obvious, call it like nearest neighbor or something that are close to a product but don't convert or something.

Okay real talk before you go down this rabbit hole — you probably can't, at least not the way you're imagining.

The Google Ads API gives you access to your own campaign data. Impression-level behavioral data on non-converters, what content they're consuming, their search patterns — that's Google's data and they don't expose it. You can get aggregate reporting, you can get your own audience insights, but the cross-advertiser behavioral signal that makes this idea actually valuable is exactly what Google walls off. That's the moat.

What you *can* actually access is conversion data, audience segment performance, and some demographic breakdowns on your own campaigns. Which is useful for optimizing your own ads but not for the market intelligence product you're describing.

The realistic version of this that you could actually build is closer to: ingest a company's own Google Ads + GA4 data via API, use an LLM to analyze patterns in near-converter behavior, and surface product/messaging insights from that. Which is genuinely useful but it's a analytics/BI product not a market research platform, and the insight quality is capped by what one advertiser's data can tell you.

The version that crushes market research requires data pooling across advertisers which requires Google's cooperation or a data clean room setup, and then you're in enterprise sales hell not a startup.

Ha. Kind of but not really — the important distinctions are:

Cambridge Analytica was harvesting data without advertiser consent through a third party exploit, targeting individuals for political manipulation, and doing it covertly. The whole thing was a scandal because the data acquisition was illegitimate and the use case was psychographic voter targeting.

What you're describing is Google selling aggregate market intelligence to advertisers using their own first party data that advertisers already consented to when they signed up for the platform, for the purpose of product strategy and market research. Completely above board, different legal structure, different use case.

The surface similarity is "use behavioral data to understand audiences" but the mechanism and ethics are totally different.

That said — the reason Cambridge Analytica was able to do what it did is because the underlying insight was correct. Behavioral data is extraordinarily predictive and the incumbents weren't using it to its full potential. CA was just the illegal, unethical, politically weaponized version of a real observation.

Which is actually a useful gut check for any data product — "is this Cambridge Analytica" is not a crazy question to ask. The answer here is no but the fact that you asked it means you're thinking about the right things. A lot of people who built data products in the 2010s didn't ask that question and it did not go well for them.
